content.text: The Shared Responsibility Model defines which security tasks Azure (the cloud provider) handles and which remain the customer's obligation. The boundary shifts depending on the service model — on-premises, IaaS, PaaS, or SaaS — but two areas are permanently the customer's responsibility regardless of model: identity and access management, and data. Understanding this boundary is prerequisite to designing secure cloud architectures, because the most common cloud security failures are not Azure platform breaches — they are customer misconfigurations in the areas they own.

attrs.alt: Azure Shared Responsibility Model matrix across IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS

content.text: What Microsoft Always Owns

Microsoft is responsible for the physical security of Azure datacenters (access controls, surveillance, physical destruction of decommissioned hardware), the global network backbone (DDoS protection at the network perimeter, redundant fiber), the hypervisor and virtualization layer that isolates tenant VMs from each other, and the firmware of host hardware. These are inaccessible to customers by design.
Physical facilities:
ISO/IEC 27001-certified datacenters with biometric access, 24/7 security personnel, and cage-level tenant isolation.
Hardware security:
Trusted Platform Module (TPM) attestation, Secure Boot, and Host Guardian Service on every Azure host. Customers cannot access host hardware directly.
Hypervisor isolation:
Azure uses a custom Hyper-V hypervisor with root partition isolation. A compromise of one tenant VM cannot read another tenant's memory.
Azure network fabric:
Microsoft controls the physical network switches, routers, and the global WAN. Customers configure virtual network overlays (VNets, NSGs, UDRs) on top.
Platform compliance:

Microsoft holds 100+ compliance certifications (ISO 27001, SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, FedRAMP, HIPAA, GDPR) for the infrastructure. These certifications cover Microsoft's controls only — customers must certify their own workloads separately.
What Customers Always Own
Regardless of whether a workload runs on IaaS VMs, PaaS App Service, or SaaS Microsoft 365, the following are always the customer's responsibility:
Accounts and identities:
who has access, under what conditions (MFA, Conditional Access policies), and with what permissions. A compromised account is a customer security failure, not a platform failure.
Data classification and labeling:

determining what data is sensitive, applying labels (Microsoft Purview sensitivity labels), and configuring encryption appropriately. Microsoft encrypts data at rest by default using Microsoft-managed keys; customers who require customer-managed keys (CMK) must explicitly configure them.
Application security:
code vulnerabilities (OWASP Top 10, injection, broken authentication) in applications deployed on Azure are the customer's responsibility. Azure does not inspect application code.
Compliance for your workload:

while Microsoft holds platform certifications, the customer must configure their workload to comply with regulations (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI DSS). For example, HIPAA requires audit logging of PHI access — Microsoft provides the tools (Azure Monitor, Log Analytics), but the customer must enable and configure them.

# Identity is ALWAYS the customer's responsibility, regardless of IaaS/PaaS/SaaS

# Enable MFA for all users via Conditional Access (required for security baseline)
# (done in Azure portal or via Microsoft Graph API)

# Check if privileged roles have MFA enforced
az ad user list \
  --filter "accountEnabled eq true" \
  --query "[].{upn:userPrincipalName, id:id}" \
  --output table

# Assign least-privilege RBAC (avoid Owner; use specific roles)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Storage Blob Data Reader' \
  --assignee user@contoso.com \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/myStorage

# List all role assignments on a resource group (audit)
az role assignment list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --include-inherited \
  --query "[].{role:roleDefinitionName, principal:principalName, scope:scope}" \
  --output table

# Enable PIM (Privileged Identity Management) for just-in-time admin access
# PIM is configured in Entra ID portal — requires Entra ID P2 license

# Customer data encryption: enable customer-managed key for Azure Storage
az storage account update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myStorage \
  --encryption-key-source Microsoft.Keyvault \
  --encryption-key-vault https://myKeyVault.vault.azure.net \
  --encryption-key-name myKey \
  --encryption-key-version ''
IaaS: Largest Customer Footprint

IaaS (Virtual Machines, VNet, managed disks) gives customers the most control and the most responsibility. Microsoft manages the physical hardware, virtualization, and host OS. Everything above the hypervisor — guest OS patching, application runtime, inbound network rules, disk encryption, host-based firewall, antivirus — is the customer's responsibility.
Customer responsibility in IaaS
Azure tool to fulfill it
OS patching and updates
Azure Update Manager, automatic VM guest patching
Host-based firewall and antivirus
Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (EDR), Windows Defender, iptables/ufw
Inbound and outbound network rules
NSG rules, Azure Firewall, Application Gateway WAF
VM disk encryption at rest
Azure Disk Encryption (BitLocker/DM-Crypt) or SSE with CMK
Authentication to VMs (SSH keys, password policy)
Entra ID-based SSH login, SSH key management, no password auth
Monitoring and alerting
Azure Monitor Agent (AMA), Log Analytics workspace, metric alerts
Application vulnerabilities
Microsoft Defender for Servers (vulnerability assessment via Qualys)
Backup and recovery
Azure Backup, Recovery Services vault with retention policies

# IaaS VM: customer is responsible for OS patching
# Enable automatic VM guest patching (Microsoft patches the OS, customer controls schedule)
az vm update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --set osProfile.linuxConfiguration.patchSettings.patchMode=AutomaticByPlatform

# IaaS VM: enable Azure Defender for endpoint (customer must explicitly enable)
az security pricing create \
  --name VirtualMachines \
  --tier Standard

# IaaS VM: enable boot diagnostics (customer must enable logging)
az vm boot-diagnostics enable \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --storage https://mydiag.blob.core.windows.net/

# Network Security Group: customer must explicitly define inbound rules for IaaS
az network nsg rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --nsg-name myNSG \
  --name AllowHTTPS \
  --protocol Tcp \
  --priority 100 \
  --source-address-prefixes Internet \
  --destination-port-ranges 443 \
  --access Allow

# Verify disk encryption (customer responsibility for data at rest)
az vm encryption show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --query '[osDisk, dataDisks]'
PaaS: Shared Network and Application Controls

PaaS services (App Service, Azure SQL, Cosmos DB, Azure Functions) relieve the customer of OS and runtime patching. Microsoft handles the underlying VM fleet, OS updates, and framework patches. However, customers retain responsibility for network access configuration (firewall rules, private endpoints, VNet integration), authentication settings, data encryption choices, and application code security.

A common misconception is that PaaS is 'secure by default.' Azure SQL Database and App Service have public endpoints enabled by default — customers must explicitly configure firewall rules, disable public access, and enable private endpoints for secure production deployments. Data in Azure SQL is encrypted at rest with TDE using Microsoft-managed keys by default; switching to customer-managed keys requires explicit configuration.

# PaaS: Microsoft patches the OS and runtime; customer is still responsible for:
# - Application code vulnerabilities
# - Network access configuration (firewall rules)
# - Identity and access (who can access the service)
# - Data classification and encryption settings

# App Service (PaaS): disable public access, use Private Endpoint
az webapp update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --set publicNetworkAccess=Disabled

# App Service: enforce HTTPS only and minimum TLS 1.2
az webapp update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --https-only true

az webapp config set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myWebApp \
  --min-tls-version 1.2

# Azure SQL (PaaS): enable Advanced Threat Protection and auditing
az sql server threat-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mySQLServer \
  --state Enabled \
  --storage-account myDiagStorage

az sql server audit-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mySQLServer \
  --state Enabled \
  --blob-storage-target-state Enabled \
  --storage-account myDiagStorage

# Azure SQL: enable TDE with customer-managed key
az sql server tde-key set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mySQLServer \
  --server-key-type AzureKeyVault \
  --kid https://myKeyVault.vault.azure.net/keys/myKey/<version>
SaaS and Compliance

For SaaS services (Microsoft 365, Dynamics 365), Microsoft manages the application layer including patching, infrastructure, and platform compliance certifications. The customer's primary responsibilities narrow to identity (managing user accounts, MFA enforcement, Conditional Access), data governance (classifying and labeling content with Microsoft Purview, configuring DLP policies), and application configuration (which sharing permissions are allowed, which external domains are trusted).

Even in SaaS, the customer is responsible for workload-level compliance. Using Microsoft 365 does not make an organization HIPAA-compliant — the organization must sign a Business Associate Agreement with Microsoft, configure audit logging, apply appropriate DLP policies for PHI, and implement required access controls. Microsoft provides the compliance tools and certifications for the platform; the customer configures and operates them.

# Check Microsoft's compliance certifications for a given region
az provider list \
  --query "[].{provider:namespace}" \
  --output table

# Microsoft Defender for Cloud: view compliance against CIS, PCI-DSS, ISO 27001
az security regulatory-compliance-standards list \
  --query "[].{standard:name, state:properties.state, passed:properties.passedControls, failed:properties.failedControls}" \
  --output table

# Customer compliance: configure diagnostic settings to export logs to Log Analytics
# (required evidence for audits: who accessed what, when)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --resource $(az storage account show -g myRG -n myStorage --query id -o tsv) \
  --name storageAuditLogs \
  --workspace $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myWorkspace --query id -o tsv) \
  --logs '[{"category":"StorageRead","enabled":true},{"category":"StorageWrite","enabled":true},{"category":"StorageDelete","enabled":true}]'

# Azure Policy: enforce customer compliance requirements (e.g., all storage must use HTTPS)
az policy assignment create \
  --name require-https-storage \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/404c3081-a854-4457-ae30-26a93ef643f9' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG

# View Azure Activity Log for audit (who did what to which resource)
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --start-time '2026-06-01T00:00:00Z' \
  --query "[?properties.statusCode=='OK'].{caller:caller, operation:operationName.localizedValue, time:eventTimestamp}" \
  --output table

The shared responsibility model is not a blame-assignment framework — it is a checklist of what you must configure to achieve security. Microsoft securing the hypervisor means nothing if you leave port 22 open to the internet on your IaaS VM with password authentication enabled.
Common Misconfiguration Patterns

The majority of cloud security incidents are not provider platform failures — they are customer misconfigurations in the areas the customer is responsible for. The most frequent patterns in Azure environments are: storage accounts with public blob access enabled, NSGs allowing RDP or SSH from 0.0.0.0/0, VMs without disk encryption, and service principals with overly broad permissions. Microsoft Defender for Cloud continuously scans for these and provides a prioritized list of recommendations.

# AUDIT: Find storage accounts with public blob access enabled (misconfiguration)
az storage account list \
  --query "[?allowBlobPublicAccess==true].{name:name, rg:resourceGroup, publicAccess:allowBlobPublicAccess}" \
  --output table

# FIX: Disable public blob access on all storage accounts in a subscription
for SA in $(az storage account list --query '[].name' -o tsv); do
  RG=$(az storage account show -n $SA --query resourceGroup -o tsv)
  az storage account update -g $RG -n $SA --allow-blob-public-access false
  echo "Disabled public blob access on $SA"
done

# AUDIT: Find NSGs that allow RDP (3389) or SSH (22) from the internet
az network nsg list --query \
  "[].{nsg:name, rg:resourceGroup, rules:securityRules[?destinationPortRange=='3389' || destinationPortRange=='22']}"

# AUDIT: Find VMs without disk encryption
az vm list \
  --query "[].{name:name, rg:resourceGroup}" \
  --output tsv | while read NAME RG; do
    STATUS=$(az vm encryption show -g $RG -n $NAME --query '[osDisk.encryptionSettings]' -o tsv 2>/dev/null || echo 'Not encrypted')
    echo "$NAME: $STATUS"
  done

# AUDIT: List all public IP addresses attached to VMs (unexpected exposure)
az network public-ip list \
  --query "[?ipConfiguration.id!=null].{name:name, ip:ipAddress, rg:resourceGroup, fqdn:dnsSettings.fqdn}" \
  --output table
Public storage accounts:

Azure Blob Storage has a public access setting that allows unauthenticated reads of any blob in a public container. This is disabled by default on new accounts since 2023, but older accounts may still have it enabled.
Overly permissive NSGs:
The default NSG in a new VNet denies all inbound traffic. But it is common to add 'allow any source' rules for RDP and SSH during development and forget to remove them in production.
Weak authentication:

Azure services support both managed identity and local credentials (connection strings, access keys). Keys never expire and are often hardcoded in application config or environment variables, creating long-lived credential exposure if leaked.
Missing audit logs:

Azure Activity Logs capture control-plane changes (who created or deleted what). Resource-level diagnostic logs (data-plane: who read or wrote data) must be enabled per-resource. Many deployments have Activity Logs but no data-plane diagnostics, leaving blind spots for compliance audits.
Using Defender for Cloud to Monitor Shared Responsibility

Microsoft Defender for Cloud (previously Azure Security Center) provides a unified security posture management view that directly maps to the shared responsibility model. Its Secure Score reflects customer-owned security controls — items that Microsoft manages (physical security, hypervisor) are not shown because they are not the customer's burden. When Secure Score shows a recommendation, it means a customer-responsibility control is not configured correctly.

# Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud across all resource types (Standard/P2 tier)
# Defender plans: VirtualMachines, SqlServers, AppServices, StorageAccounts,
#                 KeyVaults, Arm, Dns, Containers, SqlServerVirtualMachines
for PLAN in VirtualMachines SqlServers AppServices StorageAccounts KeyVaults; do
  az security pricing create --name $PLAN --tier Standard
  echo "Enabled Defender for $PLAN"
done

# View Defender for Cloud secure score (overall posture)
az security secure-scores list \
  --query "[].{initiative:name, score:properties.score.current, max:properties.score.max}" \
  --output table

# View active security recommendations (what customer must fix)
az security assessment list \
  --query "[?properties.status.code=='Unhealthy'].{name:displayName, severity:properties.metadata.severity, resource:properties.resourceDetails.resourceId}" \
  --output table

# View regulatory compliance status (shows customer-owned controls vs Microsoft-owned)
az security regulatory-compliance-standards list \
  --query "[].{standard:name, passedControls:properties.passedControls, failedControls:properties.failedControls}" \
  --output table

# Export Defender alerts to Log Analytics
az security auto-provisioning-setting update \
  --name mma \
  --auto-provision On
Enforcing Customer Responsibilities with Azure Policy

Azure Policy allows organizations to codify their shared responsibility obligations as enforceable guardrails across subscriptions. Instead of relying on manual audits, policies automatically deny non-compliant resource creation (Deny effect), audit existing resources (Audit effect), or automatically remediate non-compliance (DeployIfNotExists effect). The Azure Security Benchmark initiative contains 200+ built-in policies covering identity, network, data, and logging — the majority are in the customer-responsibility column.

# Azure Policy: enforce customer security obligations at scale

# Assign the Azure Security Benchmark initiative (covers shared responsibility areas)
az policy assignment create \
  --name azure-security-benchmark \
  --display-name 'Azure Security Benchmark' \
  --policy-set-definition '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policySetDefinitions/1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8' \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>

# Customer-authored policy: deny creation of storage accounts with public blob access
cat > deny-public-storage.json << 'JSON'
{
  "if": {
    "allOf": [
      {"field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts"},
      {"field": "Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/allowBlobPublicAccess", "equals": true}
    ]
  },
  "then": {"effect": "Deny"}
}
JSON

az policy definition create \
  --name deny-public-blob-access \
  --display-name 'Deny storage accounts with public blob access' \
  --mode All \
  --rules deny-public-storage.json

az policy assignment create \
  --name deny-public-blob \
  --policy deny-public-blob-access \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myRG

# Remediation: automatically fix non-compliant resources (DeployIfNotExists)
az policy remediation create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --policy-assignment audit-diagnostics-assignment \
  --name fix-missing-diagnostics
Responsibility Scenarios: Who Owns What

The following table walks through common incident scenarios and maps each to the responsible party. The key insight is that Azure's availability SLAs cover platform availability — not application availability, which requires the customer to design for fault tolerance across Availability Zones or regions.
Scenario
Who is responsible
What the customer must do
Azure datacenter power outage
Microsoft (availability SLA)
Design for multi-region or Availability Zones (customer architects HA)
VM guest OS has unpatched CVE exploited
Customer (IaaS OS is customer-managed)
Enable Azure Update Manager, set patch mode to AutomaticByPlatform
Azure SQL injection vulnerability in customer app
Customer (application code)
Use parameterized queries; enable Advanced Threat Protection for detection
Phishing attack steals admin credentials
Customer (identity)
Enforce MFA and Conditional Access; use PIM for privileged access
Azure Storage data exposed via public blob container
Customer (network/access config)
Disable public blob access; use SAS tokens or private endpoints
Azure platform TLS cert expires and breaks HTTPS
Microsoft (platform certificate)
No action needed — Microsoft manages platform TLS certificates
Customer TLS cert on custom domain expires
Customer (custom domain cert)
Use App Service managed certificates or automate renewal with Key Vault
Azure networking backbone route leak
Microsoft (network fabric)
No customer action required; report impact for credit
Insider threat: rogue employee deletes Azure resources
Customer (identity + RBAC)
Use RBAC least privilege, resource locks (CanNotDelete), Activity Log alerts
GDPR: personal data retained beyond legal limit
Customer (data governance)
Implement data lifecycle policies, Azure Purview data map, retention locks

Azure's SLAs for VM uptime (99.99% with Availability Zones), storage durability (99.999999999%), and database HA are Microsoft commitments. But achieving those SLAs requires the customer to use the right configuration — a single-instance VM in a single zone has a 99.9% SLA, not 99.99%.
Customer Data Protection Obligations

Data protection is one of the most nuanced areas of shared responsibility. Microsoft encrypts all data at rest in Azure storage using platform-managed keys (PMK) by default — this satisfies the provider's obligation. However, the customer is responsible for key management (deciding whether to use PMK or customer-managed keys), backup (Azure does not back up customer data automatically — it provides the tools but the customer must configure retention policies), and recovery (testing backup restoration is the customer's operational obligation, not Microsoft's).
Encryption at rest:

Azure encrypts all data at rest automatically (PMK). Customers requiring CMK must configure Key Vault integration per service. Some regulatory frameworks (FedRAMP High, some HIPAA implementations) require CMK.
Encryption in transit:
Microsoft enforces TLS for all Azure control-plane traffic. Customer-to-service transit encryption is configurable (minimum TLS version, HTTPS-only settings) — the customer must enforce it.
Backup:

Azure Backup for VMs, SQL Server, and Azure Files is opt-in. The customer must create a Recovery Services vault, define a backup policy, and enable protection per resource. Microsoft does not automatically back up customer workloads.
Soft delete:

Azure Storage, Azure SQL, and Azure Backup all support soft delete — a trash retention period before permanent deletion. Soft delete is disabled by default for Backup vaults and must be explicitly enabled by the customer.
Data residency:

Azure allows customers to select the region where their data is stored. Microsoft does not move data out of a region without customer permission (except for replication explicitly enabled by the customer, e.g., GRS). The customer is responsible for selecting the correct region for data sovereignty compliance.

# Data protection is always the customer's responsibility

# Enable Microsoft Purview data governance (catalog, classification)
az purview account create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --account-name myPurviewAccount \
  --location eastus

# Configure Azure Backup for VMs (RPO: 24h by default)
az backup vault create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myRecoveryVault \
  --location eastus

az backup policy set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vault-name myRecoveryVault \
  --policy @daily-backup-policy.json

az backup protection enable-for-vm \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vault-name myRecoveryVault \
  --vm myVM \
  --policy-name DailyBackup

# Enable soft delete for Azure Backup (protects against ransomware deletion)
az backup vault backup-properties set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vault-name myRecoveryVault \
  --soft-delete-feature-state Enable

# Configure resource lock to prevent accidental deletion of the vault
az lock create \
  --name vaultDeleteLock \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --resource-name myRecoveryVault \
  --resource-type Microsoft.RecoveryServices/vaults \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete

# Verify backup items and last backup status
az backup item list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vault-name myRecoveryVault \
  --query "[].{name:name, lastBackup:properties.lastBackupTime, health:properties.healthStatus}" \
  --output table
RBAC: Enforcing Least Privilege

Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) is the customer's primary tool for identity governance. RBAC operates at four scopes — management group, subscription, resource group, resource — and permissions are inherited downward. Assigning Owner or Contributor at the subscription scope is the most common RBAC mistake: it grants unlimited access to all resources in the subscription and should be reserved for a small number of infrastructure administrators using Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for just-in-time elevation.

Best practice is to assign built-in roles at the narrowest scope (resource group or individual resource) and create custom roles when built-in roles are too broad. For example, instead of assigning Contributor (which includes delete permissions) to a monitoring tool's service principal, assign Monitoring Reader. The customer is responsible for all RBAC assignments; Microsoft does not review or restrict customer RBAC configurations.

# RBAC: enforce least-privilege access (customer responsibility for identity)

# Audit: find users with Owner or Contributor on a subscription (too broad)
az role assignment list \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId> \
  --query "[?roleDefinitionName=='Owner' || roleDefinitionName=='Contributor'].{principal:principalName, role:roleDefinitionName}" \
  --include-inherited \
  --output table

# Create a custom role with minimal permissions (read + restart VMs only)
cat > vm-operator-role.json << 'JSON'
{
  "Name": "VM Operator",
  "IsCustom": true,
  "Description": "Can view and restart VMs but cannot create or delete them.",
  "Actions": [
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/read",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/restart/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/start/action",
    "Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/deallocate/action"
  ],
  "NotActions": [],
  "AssignableScopes": ["/subscriptions/<subId>"]
}
JSON

az role definition create --role-definition vm-operator-role.json

# Assign the custom role at resource group scope (not subscription)
az role assignment create \
  --role 'VM Operator' \
  --assignee ops-team@contoso.com \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>/resourceGroups/myProductionRG

# Remove a broad Owner assignment after scoping it down
az role assignment delete \
  --role Owner \
  --assignee ops-team@contoso.com \
  --scope /subscriptions/<subId>

# Configure an alert for when Owner role is assigned (detect privilege escalation)
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name OwnerRoleAssignedAlert \
  --scopes /subscriptions/<subId> \
  --condition category=Administrative and operationName=Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write \
  --action-group $(az monitor action-group show -g myRG -n myActionGroup --query id -o tsv)
Summary

The Shared Responsibility Model divides security obligations between Microsoft and the customer based on the service model. Microsoft always owns physical datacenter security, hardware, hypervisor isolation, and the global network fabric. Customers always own identity and access management, data classification, application security, and workload compliance — regardless of service model. IaaS places the most responsibility on customers (OS patching, network rules, disk encryption, host security). PaaS removes OS/runtime patching but customers retain network configuration, data encryption choices, and application security. SaaS narrows customer responsibility to identity, data governance, and application configuration. The most common cloud security failures are customer misconfigurations — unconfigured MFA, excessive RBAC assignments, public endpoints without firewall rules, and unencrypted storage — not platform-level breaches.
